slug: 道歉怎么道_大部分人都在假道歉
title: 道歉怎么道——大部分人都在假道歉

outline.concept: Non-apologies use specific linguistic patterns to shift blame while appearing apologetic: 'I'm sorry you felt that way' makes their feelings the problem, not your action; 'I'm sorry but...' negates with justification; 'I apologize if...' makes it conditional. The form looks like an apology; the function is escape.
outline.query: 道歉 但是 委屈 "对不起但是" "不好意思让你" 推卸 找借口

outline.concept: Self-image protection drives fake apologies. Admitting you harmed someone threatens your identity as a good person, so you craft statements that harvest the social credit of apologizing without actually surrendering accountability. The apology is for your reputation, not for them.
outline.query: 不想认错 丢面子 好人 自尊心 承认错误 困难 撑着

outline.concept: Intent ≠ impact, and 'I didn't mean to' is the most common escape hatch. Your good intent doesn't cancel their real harm. A real apology requires acknowledging that good people can cause damage, and that your intent is irrelevant to what they experienced.
outline.query: "我不是故意的" 好心办坏事 伤害 无意 结果 冤枉

outline.concept: A real apology has three necessary components: (a) name specifically what you did wrong—no vagueness; (b) acknowledge the impact on them without centering your intent; (c) commit to concrete change or repair. Missing any one, the apology fails to land. All three together are what make it costly enough to be real.
outline.query: 真诚道歉 改正 具体哪里错 承认伤害 补偿 做法 下次
